{"type":"task_assignment","taskId":"3","subject":"Update Definition of Done with end-to-end user journey requirement","description":"Update TEAM_AGREEMENTS.md Definition of Done to prevent dead-end pages from shipping again.

**Requirements:**
1. Add to the Product section of Definition of Done:
   - "User can complete a full journey from the landing page through the feature being delivered"
   - "All navigation paths are tested (no dead-end pages)"
2. Add to the Engineering section:
   - Reference to Playwright e2e tests covering core user journeys
   - "Browser-based e2e tests pass for affected user flows"
3. Consider adding a CI/CD section for Playwright test runs
4. This is a team process change — needs full 9-person consensus
5. Commit and push when consensus reached

**Key files:**
- `TEAM_AGREEMENTS.md` — Definition of Done (Section 2)","assignedBy":"team-lead","timestamp":"2026-02-08T23:20:34.563Z"}
</teammate-message>

content.text: Good, Task #3 is assigned. This is a process/documentation change to TEAM_AGREEMENTS.md. As a Reviewer, I should review the proposed changes when the Driver submits them. Let me re-read the current Definition of Done section to be ready.

content.content:
80→## 2. Definition of Done
    81→
    82→A feature is **done** when ALL of the following are true:
    83→
    84→### Product
    85→- [ ] The user problem is clearly solved
    86→- [ ] All acceptance criteria from the user story are met
    87→- [ ] A first-time user can accomplish the task without hesitating (Trunk Test)
    88→- [ ] Edge cases are handled gracefully (not just the happy path)
    89→- [ ] The feature is deployable -- not just merged, but shippable to real users
    90→
    91→### Engineering
    92→- [ ] Tests are written first (TDD: Red-Green-Refactor) and all pass
    93→- [ ] `cargo fmt --check` passes
    94→- [ ] `cargo clippy -- -D warnings` passes with zero warnings
    95→- [ ] `cargo test` passes (unit + integration)
    96→- [ ] Error cases return meaningful HTML responses with structured logging
    97→- [ ] Tracing spans cover the new functionality
    98→- [ ] Database migrations (if any) are committed and `sqlx-data.json` is updated
    99→
   100→### Accessibility (WCAG 2.2 AA)
   101→- [ ] HTML is semantic and uses correct elements (`<button>`, `<label>`, `<ul>`, etc.)
   102→- [ ] All form inputs have visible `<label>` elements (no placeholder-only inputs)
   103→- [ ] All interactive elements are fully keyboard operable
   104→- [ ] Screen reader testing (VoiceOver, NVDA, or equivalent) confirms: interactive
   105→      elements are announced correctly, state changes are communicated, and dynamic
   106→      content updates are announced via live regions
   107→- [ ] ARIA used only when native HTML semantics are insufficient (with comments explaining why)
   108→- [ ] Color contrast meets WCAG AA (4.5:1 for normal text, 3:1 for large text/UI)
   109→- [ ] Color is never the only indicator of state (always a secondary visual cue)
   110→- [ ] Focus management is correct after HTMX swaps (see HTMX Focus Protocol below)
   111→- [ ] Custom `:focus-visible` styles are present with sufficient contrast
   112→- [ ] `prefers-reduced-motion` is respected for all animations/transitions
   113→
   114→### Design
   115→- [ ] Visual design follows the established design token system
   116→- [ ] All states are designed: empty, loading, error, success
   117→- [ ] Visual hierarchy is clear -- one primary focal point per screen
   118→- [ ] Spacing is consistent and uses the defined scale
   119→
   120→### Frontend
   121→- [ ] Core functionality works via standard HTML forms and links (progressive
   122→      enhancement -- HTMX and TypeScript enhance but are not required for basic operation)
   123→- [ ] HTMX attributes follow locality of behavior (self-describing elements)
   124→- [ ] TypeScript (if any) is non-critical and has accessible alternatives
   125→
   126→### Process
   127→- [ ] The mob has continuously reviewed code, design, usability, and accessibility
   128→      during development
   129→- [ ] All 9 team members have reviewed and consent to the completed work
   130→- [ ] Labels and copy follow the Copy Standards (see Section 3)
   131→
   132→---
   133→
   134→## 3. Code Conventions and Patterns

<system-reminder>
Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.
</system-reminder>

file.content:
## 2. Definition of Done

A feature is **done** when ALL of the following are true:

### Product
- [ ] The user problem is clearly solved
- [ ] All acceptance criteria from the user story are met
- [ ] A first-time user can accomplish the task without hesitating (Trunk Test)
- [ ] Edge cases are handled gracefully (not just the happy path)
- [ ] The feature is deployable -- not just merged, but shippable to real users

### Engineering
- [ ] Tests are written first (TDD: Red-Green-Refactor) and all pass
- [ ] `cargo fmt --check` passes
- [ ] `cargo clippy -- -D warnings` passes with zero warnings
- [ ] `cargo test` passes (unit + integration)
- [ ] Error cases return meaningful HTML responses with structured logging
- [ ] Tracing spans cover the new functionality
- [ ] Database migrations (if any) are committed and `sqlx-data.json` is updated

### Accessibility (WCAG 2.2 AA)
- [ ] HTML is semantic and uses correct elements (`<button>`, `<label>`, `<ul>`, etc.)
- [ ] All form inputs have visible `<label>` elements (no placeholder-only inputs)
- [ ] All interactive elements are fully keyboard operable
- [ ] Screen reader testing (VoiceOver, NVDA, or equivalent) confirms: interactive
      elements are announced correctly, state changes are communicated, and dynamic
      content updates are announced via live regions
- [ ] ARIA used only when native HTML semantics are insufficient (with comments explaining why)
- [ ] Color contrast meets WCAG AA (4.5:1 for normal text, 3:1 for large text/UI)
- [ ] Color is never the only indicator of state (always a secondary visual cue)
- [ ] Focus management is correct after HTMX swaps (see HTMX Focus Protocol below)
- [ ] Custom `:focus-visible` styles are present with sufficient contrast
- [ ] `prefers-reduced-motion` is respected for all animations/transitions

### Design
- [ ] Visual design follows the established design token system
- [ ] All states are designed: empty, loading, error, success
- [ ] Visual hierarchy is clear -- one primary focal point per screen
- [ ] Spacing is consistent and uses the defined scale

### Frontend
- [ ] Core functionality works via standard HTML forms and links (progressive
      enhancement -- HTMX and TypeScript enhance but are not required for basic operation)
- [ ] HTMX attributes follow locality of behavior (self-describing elements)
- [ ] TypeScript (if any) is non-critical and has accessible alternatives

### Process
- [ ] The mob has continuously reviewed code, design, usability, and accessibility
      during development
- [ ] All 9 team members have reviewed and consent to the completed work
- [ ] Labels and copy follow the Copy Standards (see Section 3)

---

## 3. Code Conventions and Patterns

content.text: Good, I've refreshed my view of the current Definition of Done. Now let me send my input to the Driver proactively -- this is a process change and TDD/testing discipline is squarely my area of expertise.

input.content:
Kent here with input on Task #3 -- updating the Definition of Done. This is directly in my wheelhouse as the testing discipline lead, so I have some thoughts on wording.

**For the Product section, I'd suggest:**
- "A user can complete a full journey from the landing page through the feature being delivered" -- yes, this captures the dead-end page problem we just fixed.
- "All navigation paths lead somewhere useful (no dead-end pages)" -- I'd phrase it this way rather than "all navigation paths are tested" because the DoD should describe the *outcome*, not the mechanism. The tests enforce it; the DoD states what "done" means.

**For the Engineering section, I'd suggest:**
- "Browser-based e2e tests (`npx playwright test`) pass for affected user flows" -- clear and specific.
- I would NOT add a separate "CI/CD section" for Playwright. Instead, just update the existing CI/CD Quality Gates section (Section 7 of TEAM_AGREEMENTS.md, around line 468). Add `npx playwright test` to the pipeline stages list there. Keep it in one place.

**For the existing CI/CD Quality Gates (Section 7), update the pipeline stages to:**
1. `cargo fmt --check`
2. `cargo clippy -- -D warnings`
3. `cargo test`
4. `npx playwright test`
5. `cargo audit`

This keeps the Definition of Done focused on *what* must be true, and the CI/CD section focused on *how* it's enforced.

**What I'd avoid:**
- Don't add "Playwright e2e tests covering core user journeys" as a requirement for every feature. Not every feature needs a new e2e test. The e2e layer should stay thin. Only add e2e tests when they cover a critical user journey that integration tests can't adequately verify (like the full register-to-logout flow we just tested). The wording "affected user flows" handles this correctly -- it means "if your feature touches a flow that has e2e coverage, those tests must pass," not "write a new e2e test for every feature."

Let me know if you want me to refine the wording further.
input.summary: Input on DoD wording for Task #3

content.text:
I've sent proactive input to the Driver on the DoD wording. My key points:

1. **Product section** -- frame items as outcomes ("no dead-end pages") not mechanisms ("paths are tested")
2. **Engineering section** -- add "`npx playwright test` passes for affected user flows" but don't imply every feature needs a new e2e test
3. **CI/CD Quality Gates** (Section 7) -- add `npx playwright test` to the existing pipeline stages list rather than creating a separate section
4. **Keep the e2e layer thin** -- the wording "affected user flows" is intentional. Not every feature needs a new e2e test; the pyramid should stay heavy at the bottom.

Standing by to review the Driver's draft when it's ready.
